summary:
The Abbasid Caliphate (750–1258 AD) was the second and greatest of the Islamic dynasties — a caliphate centered in Baghdad (founded 762 AD) that presided over the [[event:2135|Islamic Golden Age]], the most brilliant intellectual and cultural flowering in the medieval world. The Abbasids came to power through the [[event:3521|Abbasid Revolution]], overthrowing the [[event:2129|Umayyads]] with support from non-Arab converts (especially Persians) who resented the Umayyad Arab privilege. The Abbasids made their caliphate explicitly more egalitarian and cosmopolitan, drawing on Persian administrative traditions and patronizing an extraordinary range of intellectual achievement.

Under caliphs like Harun al-Rashid (r. 786–809) and al-Mamun (r. 813–833), Baghdad became the largest city in the world (population perhaps 1 million) and the center of global intellectual life. The [[event:2136|House of Wisdom]] gathered scholars from across the known world to translate Greek, Persian, Indian, and Syriac texts into Arabic and to produce original scholarship. [[event:2137|Al-Khwarizmi's algebra]], [[event:2218|al-Razi's medicine]], [[event:3614|Ibn Sina's philosophy and medicine]], and [[event:3615|al-Biruni's geography]] were all products of this golden age. The Abbasid caliphate declined from the 9th century as effective power shifted to Turkish military commanders (the caliph becoming a figurehead), regional dynasties asserted independence, and the [[event:2194|Fatimids]] in Egypt challenged the Abbasids' religious authority. The caliphate ended when the Mongols under Hulagu Khan sacked Baghdad in 1258, reportedly killing the last caliph by rolling him in a rug and trampling him with horses.
